title: 2026 U.S. Open picks, odds: Expert predictions, favorites in field at Shinnecock - CBS Sports
description: The week of festivities at the 2026 U.S. Open is already underway at Shinnecock Hills with practice ongoing as tournament rounds fast ap
body:
The week of festivities at the 2026 U.S. Open is already underway at Shinnecock Hills with practice ongoing as tournament rounds fast approach in Southampton, New York. The same question around Long Island and worldwide -- outside of whether the New York Knicks can repeat as NBA champions -- is on everyone's lips: Who are you picking to hoist the trophy on Sunday evening? With an excellent 156-man field featuring the best the PGA Tour, LIV Golf and the USGA have to offer competing, the third major championship of the 2026 season should once again be a tremendous ride from Thursday's first round onward. Shinnecock Hills serving as a particularly difficult test should only heighten tensions, particularly given the high winds expected throughout all four rounds.While the U.S. Open field is stacked, the overall favorite, Scottie Scheffler, is hardly playing his best golf. While he remains a top 10 machine, Scheffler only has one win on the season -- none during the PGA Tour's most substantial tournaments. Rory McIlroy enters having won a second straight green jacket, and while his game is on point, his driving accuracy has been rough. He's also not playing as many tournaments as usual, so rust is certainly possible.Tiger Woods and Phil Mickelson will each miss their third major of the season, facing starkly different personal circumstances; it now appears neither will play the four biggest events of the year for the first time in decades.
There are, nevertheless, plenty of significant names in the field with the likes of Jon Rahm, Brooks Koepka, Cameron Young, Matt Fitzpatrick, Xander Schauffele and Tommy Fleetwood aiming to find further success on a tough USGA test.So, what is going to happen in Long Island this week? Let's take a look at a full set of predictions and picks from our CBS Sports experts as we attempt to project who will win -- and what will go down -- in the third major championship of the season.  2026 U.S. Open expert picks, predictions Odds via Caesars Sportsbook  	Patrick McDonald, golf writer  Winner -- Russell Henley (40-1): Quietly one of the best players in the world and quietly becoming a fixture in major championships, Henley has an opportunity to capitalize on his run of form at Shinnecock. The recent winner at the Charles Schwab Challenge has a sneakily good U.S. Open record and was in the thick of it on the second nine on Sunday at Augusta National. Henley is deadly accurate off the tee, precise going into the greens and has the hands and mettle to deal with the vagaries of this U.S. Open.
Sleeper – Ryan Gerard (125-1): We've got a player here who has been in the final group on Sunday in his last two tournaments; in the latter, Gerard fell just short in a playoff to J.T. Poston at the Memorial. Gaming a new confidence on the greens thanks to a small tweak in setup, Gerard is finding out that the entirety of his game is more than good enough to compete with the best in the world. He has the mental fortitude and the gumption required in U.S. Opens.Top 10 lock -- Scottie Scheffler: The man with three legs of the grand slam in his bag has finished inside the top 25 in his last 32 tournaments. Just want to make sure you see that number: 32! The mark may be the top 10 for this conversation, and despite some visible frustrations at Muirfield Village, Scheffler is still the most well-rounded and best player in the world. He is driving beautifully and possesses an underrated short game. All eyes are on the career grand slam, and the world No. 1 will put on a show.Star who definitely won't win -- Bryson DeChambeau: A little nervous with the lack of noise, but DeChambeau's shortcomings in major championships this season stemmed from loose iron play and sloppy short-game performances. Those two areas are critical to staying afloat at Shinnecock Hills. The constant wind adds another variable that the artist formerly known as the Mad Scientist will be unable to equate. 
Low amateur -- Ethan Fang: The next generation is here. So many options with Jackson Koivun, Preston Stout and the top junior in the world, Miles Russell, but we are going with Stout's teammate, Fang. The winner of the Amateur Championship last summer, Fang a.k.a "The Vampire," has an innate ability to get his ball in the hole no matter the circumstances. That is handy at a U.S. Open.Golfers finishing under par -- Two: The winner comes in at 2 under for a one-stroke victory. The USGA learned from the 2004 and 2018 championships held at Shinnecock Hills; it will set up the golf course to remain playable into the weekend even with high winds. The only concern thus far is the wind direction in practice rounds, which has yet to mirror the prevailing.Biggest surprise -- More controversy! Shinnecock Hills has become synonymous with it in the last two U.S. Opens, and let us not forget the Sam Burns ruling -- or lack thereof -- in the final round at Oakmont Country Club last season. With wind, the potential for watering greens during rounds and U.S. Open pressure, a big-time golf debate about something will break out.
Lowest round: 66 (-4)Winning score: 278 (-2)Winner's Sunday score: 70 (E)Robby Kalland, golf writerWinner -- Xander Schauffele (18-1): Did you know Schauffele's worst finish in nine U.S. Open starts is T14? That's a remarkable run of consistency at an event that is almost impossible to fake it around when you don't have your best. Schauffele has always been a major performer (19 top 10s in 36 overall major starts), and while he hasn't quite returned to the form that won him two majors in 2024, he does have back-to-back top 10s to start 2026. Shinnecock Hills requires the right mindset and a well-rounded game to get the job done. Schauffele has both. Sleeper -- Harris English (80-1): English has a tendency to pop his head up at the top of the leaderboard at majors and has a strong history at the U.S. Open. Shinnecock should fit his game well, as it demands precise ball-striking and excellent putting. Historically, he's had the ball-striking, although it's dipped a bit this year, but I don't think that skill has suddenly left him, and he's in the midst of an incredible putting season that has him sixth on the PGA Tour in strokes gained. Top 10 lock -- Jon Rahm: I'm back in on Rahm after Aronimink. That was a really tough course setup, and he played in total control of his ball from tee-to-green, which we hadn't seen him do at a proper major test in quite some time. His game is in a great place, and most importantly, he believes he is that guy again after dealing with some real doubts about his place among the world's best for the first time in his career. 
Star who definitely won't win -- Scottie Scheffler: It's no fun playing it safe and pointing out that DeChambeau in the wind has no shot. That's kicking a man while he's down. I'll be the one to say the tournament favorite will not win. Last time I tried this bit, I had Scheffler and McIlroy being outside the top 10 at the Masters, and they finished 1-2. Alas, I'm not deterred and don't think Scheffler's game is where it needs to be to win the U.S. Open at Shinnecock. The ball-striking concerns are real, and in the wind they'll face this week on greens this nasty, he will not be able to execute his usual risk-averse style well enough to complete the career grand slam. Low amateur -- Preston Stout: Koivun and Russell are the headliners getting all the buzz, but this amateur field is incredibly deep. Stout, an Oklahoma State star, who recently bested Koivun to win the individual title at the NCAA championship. Stout can benefit from the attention on Koivun and Russell by getting to just play golf this week without the spotlight, and he can make quite the name for himself with low amateur honors, given the depth of talent headed to Shinnecock this week. Golfers finishing under par -- One: Much like last year at Oakmont, the winner will come in at 1 under or maybe 2 under. The wind is going to be stiff on Thursday and Friday, but even as it lightens a touch on the weekend, the USGA has levers it can pull with the greens to keep scoring exactly where they want it. Someone will post in red figures, but the grand total of golfers to break par in a U.S. Open at Shinnecock will only move to four on Sunday evening. 
Biggest surprise -- Three players in the top 10 on the odds sheet miss the cut: That list: Scheffler, McIlroy, Rahm, Schauffele, Tommy Fleetwood, Matt Fitzpatrick, Ludvig Åberg, Cameron Young, Si Woo Kim and DeChambeau. It's tough to say which three will is it, but Shinnecock is going to take its toll and eliminate a few of the pre-tournament favorites before Saturday. Lowest round: 68 (-2)Winning score: 279 (-1)Winner's Sunday score: 70 (E)Who will win the 2026 U.S. Open, and which longshots will stun the golfing world? Visit SportsLine now to see the projected leaderboard, all from the model that's nailed 17 golf majors heading into the weekend, including the past five Masters, and find out.

title: Why Brendan Sorsby would've been QB2 in 2026 NFL Draft - CBS Sports
description: NFL teams assumed they'd have another year to figure out Brendan Sorsby. Another season of college tape, more time for area scouts to ga
body:
NFL teams assumed they'd have another year to figure out Brendan Sorsby. Another season of college tape, more time for area scouts to gather information, more opportunities to investigate the gambling history, ask uncomfortable questions and determine whether the quarterback they saw on Saturdays was someone they could ultimately trust with an organization.The supplemental draft eliminates any such luxury. It forces teams to make a decision now, whether they feel ready to or not. And after revisiting this most recent quarterback class, here's where I landed: If Sorsby had entered the 2026 NFL Draft, he would've been QB2.Not ahead of Fernando Mendoza, but ahead of everyone else. In fact, I talked to people around the league this spring who were incredibly high on Sorsby the football player and believed he would've been a first-round pick had he declared. And once I stacked Sorsby's profile against the quarterbacks who were actually drafted, it became harder and harder to justify putting him anywhere but second.Of course, his evaluation has always been more complicated than clean; the gambling history isn't theoretical, and organizations will investigate every detail and determine for themselves whether Sorsby has earned their trust. I've also heard evaluators wonder whether he loves football in the obsessive, all-consuming way the league wants its franchise quarterbacks to love it. Those issues matter, but they don't have to be the end of the discussion.
The Boutte blueprint?If you're looking for a silver lining in the incredibly dark cloud that currently hangs over the Sorsby situation, one that at times can look troubling at best and hopeless at worst for the young quarterback, the NFL has already shown that gambling problems -- even ones that involve betting on your own team -- aren't automatically disqualifying. Kayshon Boutte entered the league carrying his own gambling baggage after a Louisiana State Police investigation revealed that he placed thousands of wagers while at LSU, including betting on his own team. Charges were eventually dropped, but the headlines remained. It would've been easy for the Patriots to decide the risk wasn't worth it and move on. Instead, they stuck with him.Boutte later detailed the extent of his addiction in a Players' Tribune essay, admitting he lost roughly $90,000 and reached the point where he'd wake up in the middle of the night to place bets, describing himself as "completely broke" before football and fatherhood forced him to confront what he'd become. The Patriots didn't pretend the past never happened. They chose to evaluate the person standing in front of them rather than the worst chapter behind him.
To this point, the investment has paid off. Boutte rebuilt his reputation, carved out a role in New England's offense and became one of Drake Maye's trusted downfield targets over his first three NFL seasons. The gambling history became part of his story -- but not the entire story.Of course, the comparison only goes so far; Boutte wasn't asked to be the face of a franchise, and he didn't touch the ball on every snap. Quarterbacks occupy a different place in an organization, and teams scrutinize them differently because they have to. Fair or not, the expectations attached to the position are higher.Which means Sorsby's evaluation carries an added layer of complexity. A team isn't just deciding whether it believes the player can recover from past mistakes. It's deciding whether it's comfortable handing that player the keys to the entire operation.

        
            
                Why Brendan Sorsby may not face an NFL gambling suspension if he gets taken in the supplemental draft
                
                    John Breech                
            
                            
    
                
                    
    
The data contradictionAnd that brings the conversation back to the part evaluators can actually grade. Strip everything else away, and it comes down to this: What exactly is an organization getting if it drafts Sorsby?The answer, at least on the field, is a quarterback whose strengths compare favorably to anyone in the 2026 class. The easiest quarterback to evaluate in that group was Mendoza. Every time I watched him, I wrote down the same things. Calm. Poised. Anticipatory. Accurate. Gets through reads. Stands tall in the pocket. 
Oregon, Ohio State, Miami -- the environment changed, but the quarterback didn't. He wasn't spectacular in the way some prospects are spectacular. He simply never forced you to explain away parts of his evaluation, and the numbers reflected that steadiness.Note for the table below: Alpha measures how often quarterbacks create positive plays without offsetting them with mistakes. Deep Alpha isolates those same outcomes on throws of 20-plus yards. Pressure-to-sack percentage tracks how frequently pressure turns into drive-killing sacks, while the second-read metrics offer a glimpse into what happens once a quarterback is forced beyond his initial answer.
QBAlphaDeep AlphaP2S%Second-read gradeSecond-read comp%Fernando Mendoza2.9%23.9%18.9%65.863.9%Brendan Sorsby4.8%26.5%6.1%52.947.1%Mendoza wasn't QB1 because he dominated every category. He wasn't the best creator. He wasn't the best deep-ball thrower. He wasn't elite at avoiding sacks. He earned the top spot because there weren't many holes in his evaluation. The gap between Mendoza and Sorsby wasn't talent but certainty. Mendoza offered fewer questions, while Sorsby requires teams to weigh elite traits against some real on-field concerns.Here's what made him impossible to ignore: Sorsby finished first among the 2026 quarterbacks in Alpha Rate, first in Deep Alpha and first in pressure-to-sack percentage. He also finished last in second-read grade, a contradiction that ultimately defines his evaluation.Three of those four categories describe traits NFL teams spend years searching for. The fourth explains why he wasn't QB1. Quarterbacks who attack vertically without recklessness usually process well once the first picture disappears. Quarterbacks who struggle to move beyond their initial answer often drift into sacks, compound mistakes and sabotage drives.Sorsby did neither. He pushed the ball downfield, trusted his arm, wasn't afraid to make NFL throws and, more importantly, didn't turn pressure into disaster. Pressure-to-sack rate measures a quarterback's ability to survive. NFL defenses eventually win snaps, but the best quarterbacks minimize the damage – and Sorsby appears to understand that instinctively.Weighing the riskThe easiest way to understand why, for me, Sorsby would have been QB2 is this: There's not another quarterback in the class who had his combined on-field strengths. Mendoza processed better, Simpson operated better when getting through his reads and with anticipation throws, and Beck had stretches of cleaner football. And after Mendoza, no prospect checked every box. The question became which strengths mattered most -- and Sorsby's were difficult to find elsewhere in the class.Simpson was the closest call. When he was healthy, he had excellent tape; he played with subtle pocket movement, consistently made good decisions, had the innate feel for the game usually ascribed to "a coach's son," and the data liked him for many of the same reasons.QBAlphaDeep AlphaP2S%Second-read gradeTy Simpson2.7%18.9%18.3%74.3Brendan Sorsby4.8%26.5%6.1%52.9As it turned out, I'd already wrestled with a version of this debate six months earlier. In January, I wrote about the league's growing willingness to invest in quarterbacks who don't arrive as finished products. Simpson had only 15 career starts, and history paints a bleak picture when you use a first-round pick on a passer with that level of inexperience. Instead, the Rams, who took Simpson No. 13 overall, saw an older prospect who understood how to play the position. Les Snead and Sean McVay trust themselves to develop the parts that weren't there yet.And if one of the league's best organizations was willing to take that risk with Simpson, why couldn't another front office view Sorsby's profile through the same lens? We all know the answer: because it extends well beyond the field. Simpson's imperfections centered around his deep-ball struggles, which were at least partially explained by a midseason injury. Sorsby has football questions of his own, particularly when it comes to working through his reads, but his evaluation is complicated by far more than that. Teams also have to grapple with a sports-gambling fiasco that has seen players banned for months, years and sometimes longer.Still (and the word "still" is indeed doing a lot of work, but rest assured NFL teams will use that word frequently in upcoming meetings about whether Sorsby's a risk worth taking), the supplemental draft rarely offers access to this kind of player. This isn't a developmental lottery ticket. This isn't an athlete learning how to play quarterback. This is the quarterback who, had he declared back in January, legitimately had a chance to be QB2 in the 2026 class.At least one NFL team decided Simpson's strengths mattered more than his flaws. The supplemental draft will reveal whether there's another team out there that believes the same thing about Sorsby.If one does, it'll be investing in a quarterback whose on-field profile compares favorably to almost anyone in the 2026 class. It will also be accepting the risk that comes with everything else.
That's what makes Sorsby such a difficult evaluation. The talent is easy to see. Determining whether it's worth the baggage is the harder question.

schema.headline: Why Brendan Sorsby would've been QB2 in 2026 NFL Draft - CBS Sports
